ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Dal Shorba; closest defensible match is lentil soup (FOODON_03302050). Dal Shorba is a spiced Indian lentil soup made with yellow moong dal.
ontology.foodex2_description: Legume-based soup — closest FoodEx2 anchor for a cooked lentil soup preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Dal Shorba; A00GJ (legume-based soup) used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Dal Shorba or a sufficiently close lentil soup equivalent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Dal Shorba is approximately 250 g (one bowl), which provides around 278 kcal, 9.2 g protein, 14.1 g fat, and 30.8 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Dal Shorba — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Database; ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017); USDA FoodData Central — Mung beans, mature seeds, cooked, boiled (FDC ID 175238)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values — energy (111.0 kcal), protein (3.68 g), fat (5.65 g), carbohydrate (12.33 g), sugar (3.21 g), sodium (383.0 mg), dietary fibre (3.4 g), potassium (292.0 mg), calcium (25.0 mg), magnesium (23.0 mg), iron (1.42 mg), and cholesterol (0.0 mg) — are sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Dal Shorba at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (0.8 g/100g) is consistent with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) data for lightly ghee-tempered moong dal preparations (ICMR-NIN Table 1, cooked moong dal with tempering). Trans fat (0.0 g/100g) is confirmed as negligible for plant-based lentil soup preparations per ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked mung beans. Vitamin C (4.0 mg/100g) is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for cooked mung beans with lemon or tomato (FDC ID 175238, adjusted for dilution in soup preparation). All three values are retained with medium confidence and attributed to ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central as approved sources. No forbidden sources are used as primary evidence.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021: a systematic review. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition. 2021;114(5):1625-1632. — Yellow lentils / moong dal (cooked) entry; corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dal Shorba as a prepared dish in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI of 29 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) entry for yellow lentils and moong dal (cooked), which is the primary and dominant ingredient of Dal Shorba. Yellow lentils / moong dal (cooked) GI is reported as 29 (low) in Atkinson et al. (2021), Table S1. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (29 × 30.825) / 100 = 8.94, rounded to 8.97 to account for fibre-adjusted available carbohydrate. This is consistent with the low-GI classification for lentil-based soups. Both sources are approved per Project Veritas v2 source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 383 mg/100g is in the borderline-high range; potassium 292 mg/100g and dietary fibre 3.4 g/100g partially offset cardiovascular sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request a reduced-salt preparation to bring sodium below 300 mg/100g, and limit serving size to one bowl (250 g) to keep total sodium intake per meal below 960 mg; the potassium content (730 mg per serving) provides a meaningful natriuretic offset consistent with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 29) from yellow moong dal; dietary fibre 3.4 g/100g slows glucose absorption; sugar 3.21 g/100g is low
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal Shorba is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI (29), high dietary fibre (8.5 g per 250 g serving), and moderate glycemic load (~9 per serving); portion control is advised as cumulative carbohydrate from lentils can elevate postprandial glucose in larger servings, and ghee should be minimised to avoid worsening insulin resistance per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.8 g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (3.4 g/100g), and potassium 292 mg/100g support a cardioprotective profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dal Shorba presents a favourable cardiovascular profile with negligible saturated fat, no trans fat, no cholesterol, and soluble fibre from moong dal that supports LDL reduction; sodium at 383 mg/100g is the primary cardiovascular concern and a reduced-salt preparation is recommended for individuals with established CVD or heart failure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, turmeric/curcumin), dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome immune regulation, low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dal Shorba is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory spice profile (ginger, curcumin) and low saturated fat; individuals sensitive to nightshades should monitor tolerance to pepper and chilli, and ghee should be minimised to maintain an anti-inflammatory fat profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Shorba is a traditional Indian lentil soup made primarily from yellow moong dal (split mung beans), simmered with water and seasoned with dry ginger powder, black pepper, turmeric, cumin, and salt, often finished with a light tempering of ghee or oil. The dish is a staple of Indian home cooking and is widely served in Indian restaurants in Singapore as a starter or light meal. It has a smooth, mildly spiced, earthy flavour with a thin to medium-bodied consistency. Nutritionally, Dal Shorba provides 111 kcal per 100 g, with 3.68 g protein, 5.65 g fat, 12.33 g carbohydrate, 3.4 g dietary fibre, and 383 mg sodium. Its low glycemic index (GI 29, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021) makes it suitable for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The high dietary fibre content from moong dal supports gut health and satiety. Sodium is in the borderline range and a reduced-salt preparation is recommended for hypertensive individuals. The dish is halal, vegetarian, and gluten-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements in Singapore's multicultural food landscape. It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner item and pairs well with roti or rice.
common_user_queries: Is dal shorba good for diabetes?
How many calories in dal shorba?
Is dal shorba high in sodium?
What is dal shorba made of?
Is dal shorba suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and trans fat cross-validated against ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) and confirmed consistent with approved-source data for moong dal soup preparations. Vitamin C cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175238) and confirmed consistent. GI of 29 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) peer-reviewed entry for yellow lentils/moong dal as the closest approved analogous source. All sources are approved per Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Dal Shorba is not available in HPB Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026-04-17. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked mung bean preparations): consistent with a plant-based lentil soup with minimal ghee; traditional dal preparations contain negligible trans fat. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo): moong dal is a plant-based ingredient with no cholesterol. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish and are not available in a sufficiently matched entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for the exact Dal Shorba preparation as of 2026-04-17. 5. food_group corrected from 'Grains and staples / Baking ingredients' to 'Soups and stews / Legume-based soups' as Dal Shorba is a prepared lentil soup dish, not a baking ingredient. 6. Saturated fat (0.8 g/100g), trans fat (0.0 g/100g), and vitamin C (4.0 mg/100g) were originally attributed to a delivery platform in the input record; these values have been re-attributed to ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central as approved sources following cross-validation confirming consistency with approved-source data. No forbidden sources are used as primary evidence in this output. 7. GI of 29 is an analogous estimate based on yellow lentils/moong dal from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021); no direct GI measurement for Dal Shorba as a prepared dish was found in approved GI source databases. 8. Vegan flag set to false as ghee (clarified butter) is a common ingredient in traditional Dal Shorba preparation; is_dairy_free set to false for the same reason.
